h1Title: African Trypanosomiasis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: African trypanosomiasis, commonly called sleeping sickness, is a fatal parasitic disease caused by Trypanosoma brucei protozoa transmitted exclusively by the bite of infected tsetse flies (Glossina spp.) in sub-Saharan Africa. Two subspecies produce distinct clinical syndromes: T. b. gambiense causes a slowly progressive chronic disease with a neurological stage that develops months to years after infection, while T. b. rhodesiense causes a rapid, often fatal illness within weeks. WHO reported fewer than 1,000 cases of gambiense sleeping sickness in 2021 — down from 40,000 per year in the late 1990s — but the disease remains 100% fatal without treatment. The 2021 approval of oral fexinidazole for both stages of gambiense disease has transformed patient care in rural endemic areas.

keyStats.prevalence: 992 new cases reported globally in 2021 (WHO); down from approximately 40,000 per year in the late 1990s; 36 sub-Saharan African countries are at risk
keyStats.demographics: T. b. gambiense (98% of cases): rural communities in Central and West Africa dependent on water bodies for fishing and farming; T. b. rhodesiense: eastern and southern Africa, affecting rural workers and safari tourists
keyStats.avgAge: All ages at risk; peak in economically active adults aged 20-40 who have high tsetse-fly contact; children in hyperendemic villages increasingly affected
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 50,000-70,000 people at moderate-to-high risk in 2020; population at risk has fallen sharply with active case-finding programs

definition: African human trypanosomiasis (HAT; ICD-10: B56.9) is a vector-borne protozoan disease caused by Trypanosoma brucei gambiense (chronic, West and Central Africa) and Trypanosoma brucei rhodesiense (acute, East and Southern Africa), transmitted by the bite of infected Glossina tsetse flies. After inoculation, trypanosomes multiply at the skin bite site (chancre), disseminate to blood and lymphatics (Stage 1: hemolymphatic stage), and ultimately cross the blood-brain barrier to invade the central nervous system (Stage 2: meningoencephalitic stage). The pathological hallmark is a progressive meningoencephalitis with neuronal inflammation, demyelination, and astrocyte activation — resulting in the characteristic disturbance of the sleep-wake cycle that gives the disease its common name. The parasite evades host immunity through antigenic variation of its variant surface glycoprotein (VSG), cycling through hundreds of antigenically distinct coats to prevent clearance. Without treatment, Stage 2 disease is universally fatal; with treatment, Stage 2 gambiense disease now has 95%+ cure rates with fexinidazole or NECT (nifurtimox-eflornithine combination therapy). The disease falls within the WHO portfolio of neglected tropical diseases and is managed by infectious disease and tropical medicine specialists in collaboration with WHO and national control programs.

typesClassification.type: T. b. gambiense (West African sleeping sickness)
typesClassification.description: Chronic form accounting for 98% of reported cases. Distributed across 24 countries in West and Central Africa (DRC, Congo, CAR, Chad, Cameroon, Angola, Guinea). Incubation period weeks to months; neurological Stage 2 develops 6 months to years after infection; reservoir includes asymptomatic carriers and, rarely, domestic animals.

typesClassification.type: T. b. rhodesiense (East African sleeping sickness)
typesClassification.description: Acute zoonotic form accounting for approximately 2% of cases. Reported in 13 countries in East and Southern Africa (Uganda, Tanzania, Zambia, Malawi, Zimbabwe). Rapid progression: Stage 2 neurological disease within weeks of infection; reservoir is cattle and wild game (antelope, buffalo). Fatal within months without treatment.

Trypanosomes are confined to blood, lymph, and subcutaneous tissues. Characterized by irregular fever, trypanosomal chancre at bite site, lymphadenopathy, and constitutional symptoms. No CNS invasion; treatment is simpler and more effective.

CNS invasion produces neurological and psychiatric manifestations including sleep disorder (reversal of sleep-wake cycle — sleeping during the day, awake at night), tremor, ataxia, progressive dementia, and coma. Without treatment, Stage 2 is uniformly fatal.

primarySymptoms: Trypanosomal chancre — an indurated, painless or mildly tender skin nodule at the tsetse bite site, appearing within 2-3 days of infection and resolving over 2-3 weeks; more common and prominent in rhodesiense infection.
Irregular, recurrent fever that typically corresponds to successive waves of parasitemic trypanosome emergence as the parasite undergoes antigenic variation.
Prominent cervical lymphadenopathy — Winterbottom's sign, enlargement of the posterior cervical lymph nodes, is a classic physical examination finding in T. b. gambiense infection.

Severe daytime somnolence and nocturnal insomnia — the reversal of the normal sleep-wake cycle is the result of trypanosomal disruption of circadian regulation in the hypothalamus and is the most recognised stage 2 feature.
Progressive personality change, irritability, apathy, and psychiatric symptoms including hallucinations and manic-depressive episodes as the meningoencephalitis advances.
Tremor of the hands and tongue, cerebellar ataxia with wide-based gait, and dysarthria in advanced Stage 2 disease.
Severe pruritus and erythematous macular skin rash (trypanid) appearing in waves on the trunk and limbs, reflecting immune complex deposition.
Headache, malaise, and weight loss throughout both stages.
Facial edema, particularly periorbital puffiness, from protein-losing enteropathy and lymphatic involvement.
earlyWarningSigns: An indurated skin nodule (trypanosomal chancre) appearing at the site of a tsetse-fly bite in someone who has traveled through or lives in an endemic area of sub-Saharan Africa.
Unexplained recurrent fevers with marked posterior cervical lymph node enlargement (Winterbottom's sign) in a resident of or visitor to an endemic region.
Subtle personality change, increasing irritability, or new-onset sleep difficulty — sleeping in the daytime or being awake and restless at night — in a person from an endemic area.
Fever and constitutional symptoms in a returning traveler from East Africa (T. b. rhodesiense) that fail to respond to antimalarials and have negative malaria blood films.
emergencySigns: Coma or deep stupor — indicates end-stage meningoencephalitis; mortality in untreated late Stage 2 approaches 100% and is high even with treatment in comatose patients.

Acute cardiovascular collapse with cardiac arrhythmia — pericarditis and myocarditis occur in rhodesiense infection; electrocardiographic changes (prolonged QT, T-wave inversions) indicate cardiac involvement.

Rapid neurological deterioration within days of antiparasitic treatment (reactive encephalopathy from dying trypanosomes in the CNS) — particularly after melarsoprol therapy, now largely replaced by safer alternatives.
Post-treatment reactive encephalopathy (PTRE) after melarsoprol — seizures, loss of consciousness, and fever appearing 1-2 weeks after melarsoprol in 5-10% of treated patients; high mortality.

causes.cause: Trypanosoma brucei gambiense transmitted by Glossina palpalis group tsetse flies
causes.description: Tsetse flies of the palpalis group (G. palpalis, G. fuscipes, G. tachinoides) inhabit riverine woodland, lake shores, and gallery forest in West and Central Africa. They acquire the parasite from infected humans or rarely from animals during a blood meal; after a cyclic development in the fly gut and salivary glands over 15-35 days, infectious metacyclic trypanosomes are injected with the next bite. Humans are the main reservoir for T. b. gambiense.

causes.cause: Trypanosoma brucei rhodesiense transmitted by Glossina morsitans group tsetse flies
causes.description: Savannah tsetse flies (G. morsitans, G. pallidipes, G. swynnertoni) transmit T. b. rhodesiense in East and Southern African woodland savannah. The primary reservoir is cattle, wild buffalo, and antelope. Humans are accidental hosts, infected during agricultural, pastoral, or wildlife-tourism activities. Game parks in Uganda and Tanzania have documented tourist cases.

After hemolymphatic dissemination, trypanosomes cross the blood-brain barrier using the choroid plexus and post-capillary venules, facilitated by trypanosome-secreted metalloproteinases that degrade tight junctions. CNS invasion triggers astrocyte activation, B-cell accumulation, and diffuse neuroinflammation. The mechanism of sleep disruption involves hypothalamic hypocretin (orexin) neuron damage and prostaglandin D2 accumulation.

causes.cause: Antigenic variation of variant surface glycoprotein (VSG)
causes.description: T. brucei encodes more than 1,000 VSG genes but expresses only one at a time. Periodic switching to antigenically novel VSG coats generates successive waves of trypanosemia, each escaping the antibody response raised against the previous coat. This mechanism explains the recurring fever pattern and why the host never achieves immune clearance.

causes.cause: Imported infection via travel to endemic areas
causes.description: T. b. rhodesiense is the most common cause of sleeping sickness in international travelers. Safari tourism to national parks in Uganda, Tanzania, Kenya, Zambia, and Zimbabwe accounts for several documented imported cases per year in Europe and North America; these patients often present diagnostically challenging fever illnesses months after return.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or work in a tsetse-fly-endemic region
riskFactors.description: Rural communities in the DRC, Republic of Congo, CAR, Chad, Angola, Cameroon, Uganda, Tanzania, and Zambia near rivers, lakes, and savannah woodland have the highest endemic risk. Fishing, agriculture, and water collection bring people into repeated tsetse-fly contact.

riskFactors.factor: Safari travel or wildlife tourism in East Africa
riskFactors.description: T. b. rhodesiense is transmitted in game parks including Queen Elizabeth National Park (Uganda), South Luangwa (Zambia), and Serengeti (Tanzania). Outdoor activities at dawn and dusk when tsetse flies are most active increase exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational forestry, agriculture, or pastoralism in endemic zones
riskFactors.description: Agricultural workers clearing brush, herders moving cattle through savannah, and fishermen along lakes Victoria and Tanganyika have documented high prevalence rates in active surveillance surveys.
Men in economically active age groups with high outdoor occupational exposure carry 2-3× higher reported incidence in most endemic areas, reflecting behavior rather than biological susceptibility.

riskFactors.factor: Lack of active case-finding or remote access to healthcare
riskFactors.description: Sleeping sickness is concentrated in isolated rural communities with limited health infrastructure. Delayed diagnosis allows Stage 2 neurological disease to develop, which carries substantially higher treatment complexity and mortality.

riskFactors.factor: Proximity to cattle and wildlife reservoirs (rhodesiense)
riskFactors.description: Communities sharing land with cattle herds or living adjacent to national parks have higher T. b. rhodesiense exposure. Cattle can harbor T. b. rhodesiense for months without clinical disease and sustain transmission cycles independently of human cases.

affectedDemographics: Democratic Republic of Congo accounts for approximately 70-80% of all reported gambiense sleeping sickness cases; also endemic in Guinea, Congo, CAR, Chad, and Cameroon.
T. b. rhodesiense: Uganda, Tanzania, and Zambia account for most cases; game-reserve workers and safari tourists form a distinct exposure group.
All age groups are susceptible; children in highly endemic villages are increasingly detected in gambiense areas as adult populations receive more coverage in active surveillance.
Pregnant women with gambiense infection carry a 30-40% vertical transmission rate to the fetus; congenital sleeping sickness is often fatal.
Imported cases in non-endemic countries: fewer than 20 per year in Europe and North America, predominantly from East African rhodesiense exposure.
diagnosisOverview: African trypanosomiasis diagnosis requires both parasite detection and stage determination to guide treatment choice. A strong clinical suspicion is essential given the rarity of the disease outside endemic settings and the variable presentations. Trypanosomal chancre, Winterbottom's sign (posterior cervical lymphadenopathy), and recurrent fever in someone with tsetse fly exposure are the clinical triggers for testing. For T. b. gambiense, the card agglutination test for trypanosomiasis (CATT) is a rapid serological field test with 87-98% sensitivity that screens large populations and is positive weeks before symptoms. Parasite confirmation uses microscopy of blood (wet preparation or thick film), lymph node aspirate, or chancre aspirate — sensitivity is highest in lymph node fluid and chancre aspirate for gambiense. Mini anion exchange centrifugation technique (mAECT) and quantitative buffy coat (QBC) concentrate trypanosomes and increase microscopy sensitivity. PCR on blood or lymph fluid is the most sensitive confirmatory test but is not widely available in endemic settings. Stage determination requires lumbar puncture in all confirmed cases: CSF with more than 5 white cells/µL or any detectable trypanosomes indicates Stage 2 disease and dictates treatment choice. For T. b. rhodesiense, rhodesiense-specific recombinant antigen LATEX and serological tests are less validated; direct microscopy of blood is usually positive in the acutely ill patient. Rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) for gambiense HAT based on T. b. gambiense litaf-specific antigen (LiTat 1.3 and 1.5) have been validated for WHO pre-qualification and are increasingly replacing CATT.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid serological screening test for T. b. gambiense; detects antibody to variant-specific surface glycoproteins
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fingerprick blood sample added to a card with lyophilized trypanosomes; agglutination visible within 5 minutes. Used in mass screening of at-risk populations. Sensitivity 87-98%; positive results require parasite confirmation.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood microscopy (wet preparation, thick film, mAECT)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of trypanosomes in peripheral blood; confirms active parasitemia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood collected from a fingerprick or vein. In acute rhodesiense infection, trypanosomes are plentiful and visible on simple wet preparation within minutes. In gambiense infection, parasitemia is low; mAECT concentrates trypanosomes 20-fold and increases sensitivity.

diagnosticTests.test: Lymph node or chancre aspirate microscopy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Higher sensitivity than blood for gambiense detection, especially in Stage 1 before blood parasitemia peaks
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fine-needle aspiration of an enlarged lymph node, usually posterior cervical in gambiense infection; stained and examined under microscope. Minor procedure, mildly uncomfortable, results within 1 hour.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Determines disease stage — Stage 2 is defined by CSF white cells above 5/µL or any trypanosomes in CSF; stage determination dictates drug choice
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard lumbar puncture under sterile conditions; 15-30 minutes. CSF sent for cell count, protein, and trypanosome detection. Stage 2 CSF typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein.

diagnosticTests.test: PCR for Trypanosoma brucei DNA (blood or CSF)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive confirmatory test; available at reference laboratories; differentiates subspecies
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw or CSF sample shipped cold to a reference laboratory. Results within 24-72 hours; sensitivity exceeds direct microscopy in low-parasitemia gambiense infections.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for gambiense HAT (HAT Sero K-SeT or SD BIOLINE HAT)
diagnosticTests.purpose: WHO-validated point-of-care antibody test for gambiense HAT; replaces CATT in some programs
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fingerprick blood on lateral flow cassette; result in 20 minutes. Sensitivity 89-97% in field validation; allows diagnosis without CATT reagents in remote settings.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of African trypanosomiasis is stage-dependent and subspecies-dependent. Selecting the correct drug requires knowing both the species (gambiense or rhodesiense) and the stage (Stage 1 or Stage 2) from CSF analysis. For T. b. gambiense Stage 1 disease, oral fexinidazole (1,800 mg/day for 4 days followed by 1,200 mg/day for 6 days, all taken with a substantial meal) was approved by the European Medicines Agency in 2018 and has transformed field-level management — it achieves 91-99% cure at 18 months and requires no lumbar puncture for treatment decision in adults and children over 6. For T. b. gambiense Stage 2 disease, fexinidazole is the preferred treatment for all patients except those with CSF white cells above 100/µL or coma; such patients receive NECT (nifurtimox 15 mg/kg/day for 10 days plus eflornithine 400 mg/kg/day IV for 7 days). NECT replaced the highly toxic melarsoprol as standard Stage 2 therapy after the 2009 WHO landmark trial showing equivalent efficacy with far fewer adverse events. Melarsoprol (a trivalent arsenic compound) still has a role in T. b. rhodesiense Stage 2 disease where fexinidazole and eflornithine are not effective. For T. b. rhodesiense Stage 1, pentamidine isetionate is no longer recommended; suramin 1 g IV weekly for 5 weeks is the preferred first-line agent. Acoziborole, an oral single-dose compound, is in Phase 2/3 trials and may further simplify gambiense treatment. All treated patients require follow-up CSF analysis at 6, 12, and 24 months to confirm cure, as late recurrences can occur.

medicalTreatments.name: Fexinidazole (1,800 mg/day × 4 days then 1,200 mg/day × 6 days, oral, with food)
medicalTreatments.description: First fully oral treatment effective against both Stage 1 and moderate Stage 2 T. b. gambiense sleeping sickness. EMA-approved 2018; WHO-prequalified 2019. Must be taken with a substantial meal for adequate absorption. Not effective against T. b. rhodesiense.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 91-99% cure rate at 18 months in pivotal NECT-comparison trial (Mesu et al. Lancet 2018); now WHO first-line for gambiense HAT

medicalTreatments.name: NECT: nifurtimox (15 mg/kg/day × 10 days PO) plus eflornithine (400 mg/kg/day × 7 days IV)
medicalTreatments.description: Combination regimen for T. b. gambiense Stage 2 with CSF >100 white cells/µL or coma where fexinidazole is contraindicated. Eflornithine inhibits ornithine decarboxylase, essential for trypanosome polyamine synthesis. Nifurtimox generates cytotoxic free radicals. Requires hospitalization and IV access.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 95% cure at 18 months in the 2009 WHO NECT trial; now WHO first-line in selected severe gambiense Stage 2 cases

medicalTreatments.name: Suramin (1 g IV weekly for 5 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line Stage 1 T. b. rhodesiense treatment. A polysulfonated naphthylamine that inhibits trypanosome enzymes. Given IV in health facility; does not penetrate the CNS so is ineffective for Stage 2.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parasitological cure in over 90% of Stage 1 rhodesiense cases in African treatment programs

medicalTreatments.name: Melarsoprol (3.6 mg/kg/day IV × 3 days, 3-series protocol)
medicalTreatments.description: Trivalent arsenical still used for T. b. rhodesiense Stage 2 disease where no alternative has been validated. Enters the CNS and kills trypanosomes via disruption of trypanothione metabolism. High toxicity: reactive encephalopathy in 5-10% of patients, with 50% mortality within that group.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates 80-90% for rhodesiense Stage 2; no longer used for gambiense Stage 2 due to toxicity profile and availability of NECT/fexinidazole

medicalTreatments.name: Eflornithine (400 mg/kg/day IV × 14 days, monotherapy)
medicalTreatments.description: An older Stage 2 gambiense regimen, retained in settings where NECT supply chains are disrupted; less effective as monotherapy than NECT and requires 14-day hospitalization versus 7 days for NECT.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 80-90% cure at 12 months as monotherapy; replaced by NECT and fexinidazole in current WHO guidelines

medicalTreatments.name: Pentamidine isetionate (4 mg/kg IM, alternate days × 7 doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Formerly first-line for Stage 1 gambiense disease; still used in some programs where fexinidazole supply is limited. Requires intramuscular injection under medical supervision; common adverse effects include nephrotoxicity and hypoglycemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parasitological cure approximately 85-90% in Stage 1 gambiense disease in historical and program-level data

alternativeTreatments.name: Acoziborole (single oral dose 960 mg)
alternativeTreatments.description: A benzoxaborole compound in late-stage Phase 2/3 trial for gambiense HAT that acts against both Stage 1 and Stage 2 disease as a single oral dose. If approved, it would be the simplest treatment available and could enable community-based treatment without staging.

whySeeSpecialist: African trypanosomiasis must be managed by an infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist; treatment drugs (suramin, melarsoprol, NECT) are not available in general pharmacies and are supplied through WHO and national disease control programs. Stage determination by lumbar puncture requires specialist interpretation. Post-treatment follow-up CSF monitoring is essential to confirm cure.
doctorSelectionGuide: In endemic countries, WHO-supported national sleeping sickness control programs provide diagnosis and free treatment through designated health centers. For travelers returning to non-endemic countries with suspected rhodesiense disease, the nearest tropical medicine referral hospital or infectious disease center with access to emergency WHO essential medicine supply is essential.
hospitalCriteria: Stage 2 disease requiring IV eflornithine, NECT, or melarsoprol — requires hospitalization for full course
Coma or severe encephalopathy — ICU-level care and CNS monitoring
Melarsoprol reactive encephalopathy — requires high-dose corticosteroids and intensive neurological care
Imported rhodesiense disease requiring emergency suramin — specialist hospital with WHO-medicines access
keyFacilities: WHO-designated national HAT reference and treatment centers in endemic countries
Tropical medicine units at tertiary hospitals (Liverpool, London, MSF hospitals, Institut Pasteur)
WHO/DNDi supply hubs for fexinidazole and NECT distribution
preventionStrategies: Wear long-sleeved, long-trousered clothing in neutral colors (beige or khaki) when in tsetse-fly habitats — tsetse flies are attracted to blue and black and can bite through thin fabrics.
Apply insect repellents containing DEET to exposed skin; pyrethroids applied to clothing reduce bite frequency.
Avoid bushes, lake shores, and riverine vegetation at peak tsetse-fly activity periods (dawn and dusk).
Support or participate in mass population screening programs and vector control campaigns (insecticide-treated targets, tiny targets, and pour-on insecticide for cattle) in endemic communities.
Seek immediate medical evaluation if a trypanosomal chancre develops after travel in endemic tsetse-fly areas — early Stage 1 detection avoids Stage 2 neurological complications.
Travelers to East African game parks should inform their physician of the safari itinerary if they develop unexplained fever; rhodesiense HAT must be excluded before other causes are pursued.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full prescribed treatment course; sub-curative exposure to trypanosomicidal drugs may select for drug resistance.
Attend all post-treatment follow-up visits for CSF analysis at 6, 12, and 24 months — CSF normalization confirms cure; persistent pleocytosis indicates treatment failure.
Rest during treatment courses, especially for intravenous NECT, which requires daily hospital attendance for 7-10 days.
Report any new sleep disturbance, behavioral change, or motor symptoms in the 2 years after treatment — late relapse can be subtle.
Inform healthcare providers of prior HAT history if you develop any neurological or psychiatric symptoms in the years following treatment.

recommended: Substantial meal with each fexinidazole dose — drug bioavailability is critically dependent on co-administration with food (at least 440 kcal)
High-calorie, high-protein diet during recovery to address the weight loss and catabolism of prolonged infection
Oral rehydration solutions if nausea and vomiting accompany treatment with nifurtimox
Calcium and vitamin D if prolonged bed rest reduces sun exposure and weight-bearing
avoid: Alcohol during and for 48 hours after fexinidazole or nifurtimox therapy — both drugs inhibit acetaldehyde dehydrogenase causing disulfiram-like reaction
Fasting before fexinidazole doses — insufficient food intake substantially reduces drug absorption and cure rates
Heavy or fatty meals with suramin — not metabolized hepatically but IV administration can cause nausea if large meals precede infusion

exerciseGuidelines: Complete rest is required during the acute febrile and encephalopathic phase. Light mobilization begins once fever resolves and neurological status stabilizes. For Stage 2 survivors with persistent neurological deficits (ataxia, tremor, cognitive changes), supervised physiotherapy and occupational therapy are recommended. Return to heavy physical labor should be deferred until the 12-month follow-up CSF confirms cure.
dailyManagement: Take fexinidazole at the same time each day with a substantial meal for the full 10-day course without missing doses.
Monitor for and report CNS symptoms — new confusion, sleep disturbance, or motor symptoms — during or after treatment; these may indicate reactive encephalopathy or treatment failure.
Keep all post-treatment follow-up appointments for lumbar puncture at 6, 12, and 24 months after completing treatment.
Notify treating physicians of any new fevers or systemic symptoms during the 2-year post-treatment follow-up period.
Participate in community vector-control programs if available — insecticide-treated cattle collars and tiny targets reduce tsetse fly populations.
prognosis: Outcome depends entirely on the stage at diagnosis and subspecies. Stage 1 gambiense disease treated with fexinidazole or pentamidine achieves cure rates above 95% with no lasting sequelae. Stage 2 gambiense disease treated with fexinidazole or NECT achieves 95%+ cure at 18 months with most neurological deficits (sleep disturbance, cognitive impairment) reversing over weeks to months after successful treatment. A minority of Stage 2 survivors, particularly those with prolonged CNS disease, have persistent neuropsychiatric sequelae. Stage 2 rhodesiense disease treated with melarsoprol has cure rates of 80-90% but carries 5-10% mortality from post-treatment reactive encephalopathy. Untreated sleeping sickness is 100% fatal: gambiense over 2-3 years; rhodesiense within weeks to months. With the global case count below 1,000 for the first time in history (WHO 2021), the disease is approaching the WHO target of elimination as a public health problem, defined as fewer than 2,000 new cases per year globally.
recoveryTimeline: Stage 1 gambiense disease: systemic symptoms resolve within 1-2 weeks of completed treatment. Stage 2 gambiense disease: sleep normalization and behavioral recovery within 1-6 months; complete cognitive recovery in 6-12 months in the majority. CSF normalization (cell count below 5/µL) confirmed at 6-12 months post-treatment confirms cure. Rhodesiense disease: systemic recovery within weeks; neurological recovery variable.
complications: Untreated progression from Stage 1 to Stage 2 neurological disease — meningoencephalitis becomes irreversible and fatal within months to years without treatment.

Post-treatment reactive encephalopathy (PTRE) with melarsoprol — seizures and coma in 5-10% of treated patients with approximately 50% mortality within PTRE episodes; largely avoided by using fexinidazole or NECT.
Persistent neuropsychiatric sequelae after Stage 2 treatment — cognitive impairment, sleep disorder, and mood changes in a minority of long-treated Stage 2 patients.
Cardiac complications in T. b. rhodesiense — pericarditis, myocarditis, and arrhythmia can cause sudden death before treatment is initiated.
Congenital trypanosomiasis from vertical transmission — often fatal in neonates; all pregnant women in endemic areas should be screened.
Treatment failure and relapse — documented in 3-5% of cases; requires alternative drug selection guided by CSF monitoring at follow-up.

supportResources.name: WHO Neglected Tropical Diseases — Sleeping Sickness
supportResources.description: Epidemiology, elimination targets, diagnostic and treatment guidelines from the WHO HAT program.

supportResources.name: DNDi — Drugs for Neglected Diseases Initiative
supportResources.description: Details on fexinidazole development, acoziborole trials, and access programs for HAT treatment drugs.

supportResources.name: CDC Parasites — African Trypanosomiasis
supportResources.description: Clinical guidance for US physicians seeing imported cases; diagnostic laboratory contacts and drug access information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause recurrent fever in sub-Saharan Africa but malaria produces parasitemia detectable on a thick blood film within 7-30 days of bite, responds to artemisinin-based therapy, and does not cause Winterbottom's sign or progressive CNS disease. Sleeping sickness causes a chancre, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and leads to sleep-wake cycle inversion and dementia that malaria does not.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Advanced HIV produces progressive CNS disease, wasting, and constitutional symptoms that overlap with Stage 2 sleeping sickness. Distinguishing features: HIV diagnosis by antigen/antibody testing, absence of Winterbottom's sign and trypanosomal chancre, and different geographic distribution. Both conditions can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leishmaniasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar) causes prolonged fever, splenomegaly, and wasting in sub-Saharan Africa and is also transmitted by a vector (sandfly). Unlike sleeping sickness, leishmaniasis does not cause a neurological stage, Winterbottom's sign, or sleep-cycle inversion. Diagnosis is by splenic aspirate or rK39 antigen RDT; treatment is liposomal amphotericin B or miltefosine.

relatedConditions.name: Leishmaniasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another vector-borne protozoan neglected tropical disease in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia; shares the WHO elimination targets and DNDi drug development portfolio.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: The most important differential diagnosis for fever in tsetse-endemic sub-Saharan Africa; co-endemic and shares active case-finding programs.

relatedConditions.name: Poliomyelitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another CNS-involving infectious disease targeted by global eradication and elimination programs in overlapping sub-Saharan African geographies.

faqs.question: What is African trypanosomiasis?
faqs.answer: African trypanosomiasis, known as sleeping sickness, is a fatal parasitic disease caused by Trypanosoma brucei protozoa transmitted through tsetse-fly bites in sub-Saharan Africa. It progresses from a febrile Stage 1 (blood and lymph) to a neurological Stage 2 (brain invasion) that causes profound sleep disturbance, personality change, and coma. Without treatment, it is 100% fatal.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of sleeping sickness?
faqs.answer: Stage 1 symptoms include recurrent fever, skin chancre at the bite site, posterior cervical lymph node swelling (Winterbottom's sign), headache, and rash. Stage 2 adds progressive neurological features: daytime sleepiness, nocturnal insomnia, personality change, tremor, and eventually coma. Severe daytime somnolence despite nighttime wakefulness is the hallmark of advanced disease.

faqs.question: How is African trypanosomiasis transmitted?
faqs.answer: It is transmitted exclusively by the bite of infected tsetse flies (Glossina spp.) in sub-Saharan Africa. T. b. gambiense is mainly a human-to-fly-to-human cycle in West and Central Africa. T. b. rhodesiense is a zoonosis transmitted from cattle and game animals through savannah tsetse flies in East Africa.

faqs.question: Is sleeping sickness curable?
faqs.answer: Yes, if caught before Stage 2 neurological disease is advanced. Oral fexinidazole (10-day course) cures 91-99% of T. b. gambiense disease including Stage 2. NECT (eflornithine plus nifurtimox) cures approximately 95% of Stage 2 gambiense disease. T. b. rhodesiense Stage 2 is treated with melarsoprol, with 80-90% cure but significant toxicity risk.

faqs.question: What is the difference between T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense?
faqs.answer: T. b. gambiense causes chronic sleeping sickness in West and Central Africa, progressing to Stage 2 over months to years, accounting for 98% of cases. T. b. rhodesiense causes acute sleeping sickness in East and Southern Africa, progressing to Stage 2 within weeks; it is a zoonosis with cattle as the primary reservoir and is more rapidly fatal.

faqs.question: Why does sleeping sickness affect sleep?
faqs.answer: Trypanosomes invade the hypothalamus and brainstem after crossing the blood-brain barrier, damaging hypocretin (orexin) neurons that regulate wakefulness and accumulating prostaglandin D2, a powerful sleep-promoting molecule. The result is reversal of the normal sleep-wake cycle: patients sleep uncontrollably during the day and are wakeful and restless at night.

faqs.question: How is African trypanosomiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires detecting trypanosomes in blood, lymph node aspirate, or chancre fluid by microscopy or PCR. The CATT card agglutination test screens for gambiense antibodies. CSF analysis by lumbar puncture determines the stage: more than 5 white cells per microliter or any trypanosomes in the CSF indicates Stage 2 disease.

faqs.question: Can travelers get sleeping sickness?
faqs.answer: Yes. T. b. rhodesiense is the most common cause in travelers, acquired from tsetse-fly bites in East African game parks such as Uganda's national parks and Zambia's South Luangwa. A handful of imported cases reach European and North American hospitals each year. Any fever after an East African safari in which malaria testing is negative should prompt consideration of rhodesiense HAT.

faqs.question: What is fexinidazole?
faqs.answer: Fexinidazole is the first fully oral drug for sleeping sickness, approved by the European Medicines Agency in 2018. It is a nitroimidazole taken as a 10-day course with food. It treats both Stage 1 and moderate Stage 2 T. b. gambiense disease, eliminating the need for IV therapy and hospitalization in most patients. It is not effective against T. b. rhodesiense.

faqs.question: What is Winterbottom's sign?
faqs.answer: Winterbottom's sign is painless enlargement of the posterior cervical lymph nodes, seen in T. b. gambiense sleeping sickness. It reflects trypanosomal lymphadenopathy and was first described by British physician Thomas Winterbottom in Sierra Leone in 1803. Historically, slave traders used the sign to exclude individuals from enslavement, knowing it indicated fatal illness.

faqs.question: How many people die from sleeping sickness per year?
faqs.answer: Untreated sleeping sickness is 100% fatal. Before the modern control era, tens of thousands died annually. By 2021, case counts fell below 1,000 new cases per year globally — a milestone reflecting WHO elimination programs. Most deaths now occur in patients who reach healthcare at a late, comatose Stage 2 stage.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for sleeping sickness?
faqs.answer: No. The trypanosome's ability to switch variant surface glycoproteins (antigenic variation) across more than 1,000 different surface coat proteins makes vaccine development exceptionally difficult. Prevention relies on vector control (insecticide-treated targets, tsetse-fly suppression) and active case-finding to treat infected individuals before Stage 2.

faqs.question: How can you protect against sleeping sickness in Africa?
faqs.answer: Wear long-sleeved light-colored clothing (avoid blue or black which attract tsetse flies) and use DEET-based insect repellent. Avoid riverine woodland and game-dense areas at dawn and dusk. Game-park visitors should inform physicians of African travel if unexplained fever develops after return. No prophylactic medication is available.

faqs.question: What happens if sleeping sickness is not treated?
faqs.answer: Untreated sleeping sickness progresses inexorably to death. T. b. gambiense kills over 2-3 years through progressive neurological deterioration — dementia, coma, and multi-organ failure. T. b. rhodesiense kills within weeks to months from acute meningoencephalitis and cardiac involvement. There are no documented spontaneous recoveries.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after sleeping sickness treatment?
faqs.answer: All treated patients need lumbar puncture for CSF analysis at 6, 12, and 24 months after treatment completion. CSF white cell count below 5 per microliter at 24 months confirms cure. Persistent pleocytosis indicates treatment failure or relapse and requires rescue therapy with an alternative drug.

faqs.question: Can sleeping sickness affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children in highly endemic villages are increasingly affected as adult populations receive active surveillance coverage. Congenital transmission from infected mothers to fetuses occurs in 30-40% of pregnancies with gambiense infection, often resulting in neonatal death. Fexinidazole is approved for children aged 6 years and above weighing at least 20 kg.

metaDescription: African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness): fatal tsetse-fly disease invading the brain. Symptoms, stages, fexinidazole treatment and prognosis reviewed.
